subject: Physics
unit: Unit 1: Application of physics in other fields

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 1: Application of physics in other fields
questions.question: Astronomers measure the Doppler shift of spectral lines from a distant star. What physical quantity can be directly determined from the amount of shift?

options.B: Radial velocity (speed toward or away from Earth)

questions.hint: Doppler shift changes wavelength based on relative motion along the line of sight.
questions.explanation: The fractional wavelength shift \(\Delta\lambda/\lambda = v/c\) gives the radial velocity. Composition is from line patterns, not shift. Option C requires spectral energy distribution.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: Application of physics in other fields
source_trace.content_anchor: emission and absorption of radiation depends on the characteristics of individual atoms, and helps to measure something about the compositions, temperatures, and motions of stars by studying their spectra

explanations_tiered.definition: Doppler shift is the change in wavelength of light due to relative motion between source and observer; radial velocity \(v = c \Delta\lambda / \lambda\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: If a star moves toward Earth, spectral lines shift to shorter wavelengths (blue shift); away gives red shift. The shift magnitude gives line-of-sight speed.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The pitch of an ambulance siren changes as it passes you — that's the Doppler effect. For light, colour shifts tell us if a star is moving toward or away.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Non-relativistic Doppler: \(\Delta\lambda / \lambda_0 = v_r / c\). For known rest wavelength \(\lambda_0\), measure \(\Delta\lambda\) to find radial velocity \(v_r\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Precise radial velocity measurements (1 m/s accuracy) detect exoplanets via stellar wobble. Relativistic correction needed for high velocities (quasars).
reasoning_steps: Recall that Doppler shift changes wavelength with motion.
Identify that the shift amount is proportional to radial velocity.
Use \(v = c \Delta\lambda/\lambda_0\) to calculate speed.
misconceptions: Composition is determined from line positions at rest, not from shift.
Temperature comes from continuum shape and line ratios, not shift.

glossary_entry.definition: Change in wavelength due to relative motion; gives radial velocity.

flashcard.front: What can be directly calculated from the Doppler shift of a star's spectral lines?
flashcard.back: Radial velocity (speed toward or away from Earth) using \(v = c \Delta\lambda/\lambda\).
flashcard.teacher_note: Show examples of blue-shifted vs red-shifted absorption lines.
flashcard.learning_objective_tag: Apply Doppler effect to astronomical radial velocity measurement
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Doppler shift magnitude gives radial velocity via \(v = c\Delta\lambda/\lambda_0\).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 1: Application of physics in other fields
questions.question: The Large Hadron Collider (LHC) is a technological achievement that enabled the discovery of the Higgs boson. How did this technology contribute to new physics?

options.A: It provided a new mathematical framework
options.B: It allowed experiments at previously unattainable energies, testing theoretical predictions
options.C: It replaced the need for theoretical physics
options.D: It only improved existing measurements without new discoveries

questions.hint: Higher energy particle collisions can produce previously unseen particles.
questions.explanation: The LHC accelerates protons to TeV energies, enabling production of massive particles like the Higgs boson, predicted by the Standard Model. Option C is false; theory guided the search.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: Application of physics in other fields
source_trace.content_anchor: The rocket technology allowing blasting off from the earth enables us to take scientific measurements in space. The discovery of X‑ray technology helped in further development of physics.

explanations_tiered.definition: Particle colliders are technological tools that accelerate particles to high energies, creating conditions to study fundamental forces and discover new particles.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The LHC reached 13 TeV center-of-mass energy, sufficient to produce Higgs bosons (mass ~125 GeV/c²) via \(E = mc^2\). This confirmed the Brout-Englert-Higgs mechanism.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine smashing two clocks together so hard that the pieces form a new, never-before-seen tiny gear — the LHC does that with protons to discover new particles.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The Higgs boson was predicted in 1964. The LHC's high luminosity and energy allowed observation of decay modes H→γγ, H→ZZ*→4l, confirming existence.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The LHC's superconducting RF cavities and dipole magnets (8.3 T) are engineering marvels. Discovery of the Higgs boson (2012) earned Nobel Prize; technology enabled physics beyond Standard Model searches.
reasoning_steps: Recall that new particles require high energy to create (E=mc²).
Technology (LHC) provides the required energy.
Discovery of Higgs boson confirmed theory.
misconceptions: Technology does not replace theory; theory guides where to look (Higgs mass range predicted).
The LHC made new discoveries, not just improved old measurements.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Technology (LHC) → TeV energies → Higgs boson discovery → new physics.

glossary_entry.definition: Elementary particle giving mass to others, discovered at LHC in 2012.

flashcard.front: How did the LHC technology lead to new physics discovery?
flashcard.back: It reached high enough energies (TeV) to produce predicted particles like the Higgs boson via \(E=mc^2\).
flashcard.glossary_definition: Particle accelerator that discovered Higgs boson.
flashcard.revision_note: LHC: high energy → new particle discovery.
flashcard.teacher_note: Discuss the interplay: theory predicts, technology enables experimental verification.
flashcard.learning_objective_tag: Explain how technology enables new physics discoveries
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The LHC achieved unprecedented energies, allowing experimental confirmation of the Higgs boson, predicted by theory.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 1: Application of physics in other fields
questions.question: Some people worry that radiation therapy might cause secondary cancers. Why can high-energy radiation damage healthy tissue even though it is aimed at the tumor?

options.A: Radiation always travels in straight lines only
options.B: The beam must pass through healthy tissue to reach deep tumors, causing some damage
options.C: Healthy tissue does not repair any damage
options.D: Radiation is absorbed only by cancer cells

questions.hint: Radiation cannot skip over healthy tissue in its path.
questions.explanation: External beam radiation passes through skin and other normal tissues before reaching the tumor, depositing some dose. Option D is false; all cells absorb radiation.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: Application of physics in other fields
source_trace.content_anchor: the effect of radiation on the healthy tissue can be reduced by frequently altering the direction of the beam

explanations_tiered.definition: External beam radiation therapy uses ionizing radiation directed at a tumor; the beam unavoidably passes through intervening healthy tissues.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Radiation dose is deposited along the entire beam path. While the tumor receives the prescribed dose, entry and exit paths also receive lower doses, risking long-term damage.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you spray a hose at a weed, water also hits the flowers in front. Radiation is similar — it goes through healthy skin and organs to reach the deep tumor.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Beam attenuation follows exponential law: \(I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}\). To deliver high dose to deep tumor, incident intensity must be high, so superficial tissues receive significant dose.
explanations_tiered.advanced: IMRT and proton therapy reduce healthy tissue dose. Protons have a Bragg peak, depositing most energy at depth, minimizing exit dose. Fractionation allows healthy tissue repair.
reasoning_steps: Recall that radiation travels in straight lines and penetrates tissue.
Beam must pass through entry path to reach target.
Healthy tissue along path absorbs some radiation.
misconceptions: Radiation does not skip healthy tissue; it affects everything along its path.
Healthy tissue does repair damage (hence fractionation works), just less effectively than cancer cells.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Radiotherapy: beam passes through healthy tissue → some unavoidable damage.

glossary_entry.definition: Cancer treatment using radiation directed from outside the body; passes through healthy tissue to reach tumor.

flashcard.front: Why does radiation therapy sometimes damage healthy tissue near the tumor site?
flashcard.back: The beam must pass through healthy tissue to reach the deep tumor, depositing some radiation dose along the path.
flashcard.glossary_definition: Radiation passes through skin and organs to treat deep tumors.
flashcard.revision_note: Radiation path unavoidably hits healthy tissue.
flashcard.teacher_note: Explain multiple-field techniques to spread dose to healthy tissue over a larger volume, reducing maximum dose to any single area.
flashcard.learning_objective_tag: Explain physical basis of radiation therapy tissue damage
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The radiation beam must pass through healthy tissue to reach the tumor, unavoidably depositing dose along the path.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 1: Application of physics in other fields
questions.subsection: Infra-red wave detection for night vision
questions.structural_anchor: Infra-red wave detection for night vision
questions.question: Night vision goggles using thermal imaging can detect a warm human body even in complete darkness. What physical law describes the relationship between temperature and the intensity of emitted infrared radiation?
questions.hint: Total radiated power per unit area increases with temperature to the fourth power.
questions.explanation: Stefan-Boltzmann law: \(P = \epsilon \sigma A T^4\). Warmer objects emit more total IR power. Wien's law gives peak wavelength, not total intensity.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: Application of physics in other fields
source_trace.subsection_anchor: Infra-red wave detection for night vision
source_trace.structural_element: Infra-red wave detection for night vision
source_trace.content_anchor: All people, places, and things give off infrared light in an amount proportional to their temperature

explanations_tiered.definition: The Stefan-Boltzmann law states that the total power radiated per unit area of a black body is proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Human body temperature (~310 K) emits thermal IR. The total radiated power per unit area is \(j^* = \epsilon \sigma T^4\), so a warmer body appears brighter in thermal imaging.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A hot stove glows red, but even warm objects give off invisible heat rays. The hotter they are, the more heat rays they emit — that's the Stefan-Boltzmann law.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Stefan-Boltzmann: \(P = \epsilon \sigma A T^4\). For small temperature differences, the sensor detects contrast as \(\Delta P \propto 4\epsilon \sigma A T^3 \Delta T\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: For human skin (\(\epsilon \approx 0.98\) in mid-IR), emitted power ≈ 500 W/m². Modern uncooled microbolometers have noise equivalent temperature difference (NETD) < 50 mK.
reasoning_steps: Recall that total IR emission power increases with temperature.
Stefan-Boltzmann gives power ∝ T⁴.
Thermal imagers detect this emitted power.
misconceptions: Wien's law gives peak wavelength, not total power; it does not directly describe how brightness changes with temperature.
Planck's law is spectral distribution, not total intensity.

glossary_entry.definition: Total radiant power from a black body is proportional to T⁴; used in thermal imaging.

flashcard.front: Which law relates total emitted infrared intensity to an object's temperature for thermal night vision?
flashcard.glossary_definition: Radiated power increases with T⁴, enabling thermal detection.
flashcard.teacher_note: Contrast with Wien's law: T gives peak wavelength (for spectral filtering).
flashcard.learning_objective_tag: Apply Stefan-Boltzmann law to thermal imaging
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Stefan-Boltzmann law: total emitted IR power per unit area ∝ T⁴, giving temperature contrast in thermal images.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 1: Application of physics in other fields
questions.question: Mobile phones communicate with cell towers using radio waves. What physical phenomenon allows signals to reach a mobile phone even when there is no direct line of sight due to buildings?
questions.hint: Waves can bend around obstacles when the obstacle size is comparable to wavelength.
questions.explanation: Diffraction allows radio waves to bend around building edges and reach shadowed areas. Refraction changes direction due to density variation, not main cause for building blockage.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: Application of physics in other fields
source_trace.content_anchor: The knowledge of electromagnetic theory is crucial to understand radio waves, microwaves, infrared waves and visible light which are used in wireless and fiber optics communication

explanations_tiered.definition: Diffraction is the bending of waves around obstacles or through openings, allowing propagation into shadow regions.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Radio wavelengths (10 cm to 10 m) are comparable to building sizes, causing significant diffraction. This creates signal coverage behind obstacles.
explanations_tiered.beginner: You can hear someone around a corner even if you can't see them — sound bends. Radio waves also bend around buildings, so your phone still works.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Huygens-Fresnel principle: each point on a wavefront is a secondary source. When blocked, these sources produce diffracted fields into geometric shadow.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Fresnel zones: if obstacles block less than 40% of the first Fresnel zone, diffraction loss is small. Knife-edge diffraction models (e.g., Fresnel integral) predict field strength behind buildings.
reasoning_steps: Recall that direct line-of-sight is blocked by buildings.
Waves can bend around obstacles (diffraction).
Diffraction enables non-line-of-sight communication.
misconceptions: Refraction occurs in atmosphere due to temperature gradients, but it does not primarily account for bending around individual buildings.
Polarization affects orientation, not bending.

glossary_entry.definition: Bending of waves around obstacles; allows radio signals to reach non-line-of-sight areas.

flashcard.front: How can a mobile phone receive a signal when a building blocks the direct line of sight to the cell tower?
flashcard.back: Radio waves diffract (bend) around the building, allowing the signal to reach the phone.
flashcard.teacher_note: Demonstrate with a ripple tank and obstacle; show bending of waves behind the obstacle.
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Diffraction causes radio waves to bend around obstacles, providing coverage without direct line of sight.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 1: Application of physics in other fields
questions.question: A saltwater solution is heated until only salt remains. Which physics concept best explains why the water escapes but the salt does not?

options.A: Water has a lower specific heat capacity
options.B: Water molecules are volatile and have higher vapor pressure at the same temperature compared to salt ions

questions.explanation: Water has significant vapor pressure at the boiling point; salt ions are non-volatile and remain. Option C is false; salt does not sublime under normal conditions.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: Application of physics in other fields
source_trace.content_anchor: separation of dissolved salt from water by evaporation method

explanations_tiered.definition: Vapor pressure is the pressure exerted by a vapor in equilibrium with its liquid at a given temperature; volatile substances have high vapor pressure.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Water molecules at the surface can escape into the gas phase because water has high vapor pressure near boiling. Salt ions require much higher temperatures to evaporate.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Water 'likes' to turn into steam when heated; salt 'likes' to stay solid even at 100°C. That's why evaporation separates them.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: At 100°C, water vapor pressure = 1 atm. Salt (NaCl) vapor pressure is negligible until ~1465°C. Thus only water evaporates.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Raoult's law: \(P_{\text{solvent}} = X_{\text{solvent}} P^0_{\text{solvent}}\). For dilute salt solution, \(X_{\text{water}} \approx 1\), so water vapor pressure still high; salt non-volatile.
reasoning_steps: Recognise that evaporation occurs when molecules escape liquid.
Water molecules have enough kinetic energy at 100°C to overcome intermolecular forces.
Salt ions have extremely low vapor pressure at that temperature.
misconceptions: Specific heat capacity affects heating rate, not whether a substance evaporates.
Salt does not sublime at 100°C; it melts at 801°C and boils much higher.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Evaporation separation: water volatile (high vapor pressure), salt non-volatile.

glossary_entry.definition: Tendency of a substance to vaporize; water is volatile, salt is non-volatile at 100°C.

flashcard.front: Why does water evaporate but salt remains when a salt solution is boiled?
flashcard.back: Water is volatile (high vapor pressure at 100°C); salt is non-volatile (negligible vapor pressure).
flashcard.glossary_definition: Ease of evaporation; high for water, low for salt.
flashcard.teacher_note: Relate to distillation: separation based on volatility differences.
flashcard.learning_objective_tag: Explain evaporation separation using vapor pressure concept
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Water has high vapor pressure at 100°C and evaporates; salt has negligible vapor pressure at this temperature, so it remains.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 1: Application of physics in other fields
questions.question: In MRI, a radiofrequency pulse is applied at the Larmor frequency to flip hydrogen proton spins. What determines the Larmor frequency for a given magnetic field strength?

options.B: The gyromagnetic ratio of the proton

questions.hint: The resonance frequency depends on the type of nucleus and the magnetic field.
questions.explanation: Larmor frequency \(\omega = \gamma B_0\), where \(\gamma\) is the gyromagnetic ratio (42.58 MHz/T for hydrogen). Option A is not correct; \(\gamma\) is nucleus-specific.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: Application of physics in other fields
source_trace.content_anchor: How do we get an image from these oscillation of flipped hydrogen protons in the brain?

explanations_tiered.definition: The Larmor frequency is the precession frequency of magnetic moments in a static magnetic field, given by \(\omega = \gamma B_0\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The gyromagnetic ratio \(\gamma\) is a constant for each nuclear species. For hydrogen (¹H), \(\gamma/2\pi = 42.58\) MHz/T. Thus frequency depends on \(B_0\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Each type of atomic nucleus spins at its own rate in a magnetic field — like a top with its own wobble speed. The Larmor frequency is that speed, and it's different for hydrogen vs. other atoms.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For ¹H in a 1.5 T MRI scanner, \(f = 42.58 \times 1.5 \approx 63.9\) MHz. The RF pulse must match this frequency to flip spins.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The gyromagnetic ratio \(\gamma = g e/(2m_p)\) where g ≈ 5.586 for proton. Chemical shift (few ppm) arises from local electron shielding, slightly altering effective \(B_0\).
reasoning_steps: Recall Larmor equation: ω = γ B₀.
γ is fixed for each nucleus (property of the proton).
Thus for given B₀, ω is determined by γ.
misconceptions: Proton mass does not directly appear; γ accounts for charge and mass.
Temperature affects relaxation times, not the resonant frequency.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Larmor frequency: ω = γ B₀, γ for hydrogen = 42.58 MHz/T.

glossary_entry.definition: Ratio of magnetic moment to angular momentum; determines Larmor frequency for a given B₀.

flashcard.front: In MRI, what constant determines the Larmor frequency for hydrogen protons at a given magnetic field strength?
flashcard.back: The gyromagnetic ratio (γ) of hydrogen: ω = γ B₀.
flashcard.glossary_definition: Nucleus-specific constant linking frequency to magnetic field in MRI.
flashcard.teacher_note: Show that different nuclei have different γ; MRI specifically images hydrogen because of its high abundance and large γ.
flashcard.learning_objective_tag: Explain Larmor frequency in MRI using gyromagnetic ratio
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The Larmor frequency ω = γB₀, where γ is the gyromagnetic ratio, a constant for each type of nucleus.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 1: Application of physics in other fields
questions.question: Geologists use magnetometers to measure variations in Earth's magnetic field. What geological feature might cause a local magnetic anomaly?
questions.hint: Magnetic anomalies are caused by ferromagnetic or strongly magnetic minerals.
questions.explanation: Magnetite (Fe₃O₄) is strongly magnetic and can produce significant local magnetic field variations. Salt, water, and sandstone have negligible magnetic susceptibility.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: Application of physics in other fields

explanations_tiered.definition: Magnetic anomaly is a local variation in Earth's magnetic field caused by magnetic minerals in the subsurface.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Magnetite and other ferrimagnetic minerals have high magnetic susceptibility, distorting the Earth's magnetic field. Magnetometers detect these anomalies to map ore bodies.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A compass needle swings toward a hidden iron deposit — that's a magnetic anomaly. Geologists use sensitive compasses (magnetometers) to find such deposits.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Magnetic susceptibility κ measures a material's magnetization in an applied field. Salt, water, quartz: κ ≈ 0. Magnetite: κ ~ 0.1−1 (SI units).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Total magnetic field anomaly ΔT = \(\frac{\mu_0 m}{4\pi r^3} (2\cos\theta)\) for a dipole source. Magnetic surveys (airborne or ground) locate buried magnetite bodies.
reasoning_steps: Recall that only certain minerals are magnetic.
Magnetite is a common magnetic mineral.
Magnetic anomalies indicate presence of such minerals.
misconceptions: Salt, water, and sandstone are non-magnetic; they do not cause anomalies.
Magnetometers measure field changes, not gravity or conductivity.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Magnetic anomaly → magnetite or other magnetic minerals.

glossary_entry.definition: Local disturbance in Earth's magnetic field due to subsurface magnetic minerals.

flashcard.front: What type of mineral deposit could cause a local magnetic anomaly detected by a magnetometer?
flashcard.back: Magnetite-rich ore bodies (ferrimagnetic minerals) cause magnetic anomalies.
flashcard.glossary_definition: Field disturbance from magnetic minerals like magnetite.
flashcard.teacher_note: Show example magnetic anomaly map over a known iron deposit.
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Magnetite is strongly magnetic, producing detectable anomalies; other listed materials are non-magnetic.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 1: Application of physics in other fields
questions.question: A transformer changes voltage levels in AC circuits using electromagnetic induction. Which of the following is NOT correctly described by the ideal transformer equation?

options.B: Current ratio equals inverse turns ratio
options.D: Frequency is changed by the turns ratio

questions.hint: Transformers do not change frequency; frequency stays the same.
questions.explanation: In an ideal transformer, \(V_p/V_s = N_p/N_s\), \(I_p/I_s = N_s/N_p\), and power in = power out. Frequency is unchanged. Option D is false.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: Application of physics in other fields
source_trace.content_anchor: Electrical engineering demands the understanding of some physics concepts like electromagnetism

explanations_tiered.definition: An ideal transformer changes voltage and current levels while keeping power and frequency constant; it obeys \(V_p/V_s = N_p/N_s\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Faraday's law induces voltage proportional to number of turns. Frequency is determined by the AC source and is unchanged by the transformer.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A transformer changes voltage up or down, but the electricity still alternates at the same speed (frequency) — like changing gear ratios without changing how fast you pedal.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For a sinusoidal input \(V_p = V_{p0} \sin(2\pi ft)\), output is \(V_s = (N_s/N_p) V_{p0} \sin(2\pi ft)\). Same f.
explanations_tiered.advanced: In a real transformer, frequency affects core losses (hysteresis, eddy currents), but the ideal model assumes perfect coupling and no frequency change.
reasoning_steps: Recall ideal transformer relationships.
Note that voltage and current ratios involve turns ratio.
Frequency is determined by the source, not the transformer.
misconceptions: Some think transformers can change frequency; they cannot in ideal case.
Power is conserved, not transformed in frequency.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Ideal transformer: V ratio = N ratio, I ratio = 1/N ratio, P same, f same.

glossary_entry.definition: Lossless transformer with perfect coupling; changes V and I but not frequency or power.

flashcard.front: Which quantity is NOT changed by an ideal transformer?
flashcard.glossary_definition: Device that changes voltage/current but not frequency or power.
flashcard.teacher_note: Contrast with frequency changers which use motor-generator sets or power electronics.
flashcard.solution_manual_note: Answer D. An ideal transformer does not change frequency; frequency remains the same as the input AC source.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 1: Application of physics in other fields
questions.question: A radar transmits a pulse and receives its echo after 300 μs. What is the range of the target? (c = 3.0 × 10⁸ m/s)
questions.explanation: t = 300 μs = 3.0 × 10⁻⁴ s. Total distance = c t = 9.0 × 10⁴ m = 90 km. Range = 45 km. Option B is total distance, not one-way.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 1: Application of physics in other fields

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Given t = 3×10⁻⁴ s, total distance = 9×10⁴ m = 90 km. Half of that is the one-way range: 45 km.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If your echo comes back in 300 μs, the sound went out and back. Divide total distance by 2 to get the one-way distance to the target.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For a pulse radar, the maximum unambiguous range is c·PRT/2. Here 300 μs corresponds to 45 km range, well within typical air surveillance radar limits.
worked_solution: Step 1: Write formula R = c t / 2.
reasoning_steps: Convert microseconds to seconds.
Calculate total round-trip distance.
Divide by 2 to get one-way range.
misconceptions: Forgetting to divide by 2 gives 90 km (option B), which is the round-trip distance.
Using t as one-way would give 45 km but that's incorrect timing; the given t is round-trip.

glossary_entry.definition: Distance to target = (speed of light × round-trip time)/2.

flashcard.front: Radar echo delay = 300 μs. Range? (c=3×10⁸ m/s)
flashcard.teacher_note: Reinforce factor of 2: common mistake is to forget half.
flashcard.learning_objective_tag: Calculate radar range from time delay
